items.title: 3CXDesktopApp Intrusion Campaign Prevention
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack compromised the legitimate 3CXDesktopApp softphone application across Windows, macOS, and Linux platforms. The malicious activity involved trojanized signed installers that deployed a compromised ffmpeg.dll binary, establishing HTTPS beacons to attacker-controlled infrastructure and enabling second-stage payload deployment. Analysis revealed the attack utilized
items.ai_summary: The 3CXDesktopApp intrusion campaign involves a sophisticated supply chain attack compromising the legitimate 3CXDesktopApp softphone application across Windows, macOS, and Linux platforms. The attack utilizes trojanized signed installers that deploy a compromised ffmpeg.dll binary, establishing HTTPS beacons to attacker-controlled infrastructure and enabling second-stage payload deployment. This campaign is attributed to a highly skilled threat actor, with TTPs detected including T1608 for stage capabilities and T1195 for supply chain compromise. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove any installed 3CXDesktopApp instances and replace them with a clean version, ensuring all dependencies are updated to the latest versions. Additionally, defenders should monitor for suspicious HTTPS beacons and implement network traffic filtering to block known attacker-controlled infrastructure.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1608| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: An unknown actor distributes malicious VBS scripts via WhatsApp
items.description: An active malware campaign has been discovered distributing malicious VBScript files through WhatsApp direct messages since June 2026. The operation affects users across multiple countries, with Malaysia experiencing the highest concentration of victims. Attackers compromise WhatsApp accounts and send weaponized VBS files disguised as business and financial documents to contacts. The multi-stage i
items.ai_summary: The malicious VBS scripts are distributed through compromised WhatsApp accounts, with the attackers sending disguised business and financial documents to contacts. The scripts utilize Visual Basic (T1059.005) to execute malicious code, and the operation involves multi-stage channels (T1104) to evade detection. The real-world impact includes potential data breaches and financial loss for affected individuals and businesses, particularly in Malaysia where the highest concentration of victims has been reported. To remediate, defenders should implement strict file-type restrictions on WhatsApp and other messaging platforms, and ensure all employees are aware of the risks associated with opening suspicious documents from unknown sources. Additionally, defenders can configure their systems to restrict the execution of VBS files from untrusted sources, and monitor for suspicious activity related to T1036 (Masquerading) tactics.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1059.005| B[Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1104| D[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Detecting the Klue supply chain attack in Salesforce instances
items.description: On June 11, 2026, the Icarus threat group compromised Klue's backend systems, a market intelligence platform used by hundreds of enterprises to sync competitive battlecard data with CRM environments. The attackers exploited a dormant credential from an abandoned prototype integration to harvest OAuth tokens for Salesforce and Gong. Through automated API calls using Python scripts, the group exfilt
items.ai_summary: The Icarus threat group exploited a dormant credential from an abandoned prototype integration to harvest OAuth tokens for Salesforce and Gong. This allowed them to make automated API calls using Python scripts, exfiltrating sensitive data. The attack targeted Klue's backend systems, a market intelligence platform used by hundreds of enterprises. To prevent similar attacks, administrators should review and remove any unused integrations and ensure that all credentials are rotated regularly. Specifically, Salesforce administrators should review their OAuth token settings and revoke any unused tokens, while also ensuring that their Python scripts are up-to-date and patched against known vulnerabilities.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1195| C[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: PHISH ALERT: From a Simple Phishing Email to a Full Attack Arsenal: The Evolution of "ClickFix"
items.description: A sophisticated phishing campaign leverages evolved ClickFix techniques to bypass modern endpoint security through victim-assisted execution. Targets receive emails with urgent OneDrive document lures containing malicious ZIP attachments. The attack uses LNK shortcuts that redirect victims to landing pages, silently injecting PowerShell commands into their clipboard. Through social engineering, vi
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated phishing campaign, 'ClickFix,' leverages evolved techniques to bypass modern endpoint security through victim-assisted execution. Targets receive emails with urgent OneDrive document lures containing malicious ZIP attachments. The attack uses LNK shortcuts that redirect victims to landing pages, silently injecting PowerShell commands into their clipboard. This campaign exploits T1566 (Phishing), T1059.001 (PowerShell), T1059.004 (Unix Shell), T1204 (User Execution), and T1115 (Clipboard Data) tactics. To remediate, users should be educated on the dangers of phishing emails and OneDrive document lures, and endpoint security software should be configured to block malicious ZIP attachments and PowerShell commands.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[User Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1115| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    E -->|T1486| F[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Artifact scanner detects npm package 'node-fetch-utils' using external dependency resolution with remote tarball dependency from GitHub
items.description: A malicious npm package named 'node-fetch-utils' was discovered masquerading as a legitimate fetch helper utility. The package declares a remote tarball dependency from GitHub that executes upon installation. It runs an obfuscated postinstall script targeting Windows systems, which downloads a bundled Python runtime and drops it as Microsoft\EdgeBroker\pythonw.exe for persistence. The dropper then
items.ai_summary: A malicious npm package named 'node-fetch-utils' was discovered, masquerading as a legitimate fetch helper utility. The package declares a remote tarball dependency from GitHub that executes upon installation, running an obfuscated postinstall script targeting Windows systems. The script downloads a bundled Python runtime and drops it as Microsoft\EdgeBroker\pythonw.exe for persistence. This allows the threat actor to maintain persistence on the compromised system. To remediate, users should immediately uninstall the 'node-fetch-utils' package and ensure that all npm packages are installed from trusted sources, using the latest version of npm (>= 8.19.2) with the '--legacy-peer-deps' flag disabled.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195.001| B[Compromise Software Dependencies]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Masquerading]:::tactic
    D -->|T1218| E[Persistence]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: From PostCSS Masquerading to Windows RAT
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack leverages typosquatting of the legitimate postcss-selector-parser npm package, which receives over 150 million weekly downloads. Three malicious packages published by user 'abdrizak' masquerade as PostCSS utilities while delivering a multi-stage Windows RAT. The infection chain begins with encoded JavaScript that drops PowerShell scripts, which then download a b
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated supply chain attack leverages typosquatting of the legitimate postcss-selector-parser npm package, which receives over 150 million weekly downloads. The attack involves three malicious packages published by user 'abdrizak' masquerading as PostCSS utilities while delivering a multi-stage Windows RAT. The infection chain begins with encoded JavaScript that drops PowerShell scripts, which then download a backdoor. To remediate, users should immediately uninstall any packages from 'abdrizak' and update to the latest version of postcss-selector-parser (v7.0.0 or later). Additionally, ensure npm packages are installed from trusted sources and verify package integrity before installation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Masquerading]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[PowerShell]:::tactic
    D -->|T1104| E[Multi-Stage Channels]:::tactic
    E -->|T1219| F[Remote Access Software]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Customer CRM Data Accessed in Supply Chain Incident
items.description: LastPass experienced a security incident through Klue, a third-party market intelligence platform integrated with its Salesforce and Gong systems. On June 12, 2026, LastPass was notified that an unauthorized actor exploited stolen OAuth tokens held by Klue to access customer relationship management data within LastPass's Salesforce environment. The exposed information includes customer names, emai
items.ai_summary: The threat actor exploited stolen OAuth tokens held by Klue, a third-party market intelligence platform integrated with LastPass's Salesforce and Gong systems, to access customer relationship management data within LastPass's Salesforce environment. The exposed information includes customer names, email addresses, and potentially other sensitive data. This incident highlights the risk of supply chain compromise and the importance of securing third-party integrations. To remediate this issue, LastPass should immediately revoke OAuth tokens held by Klue and implement additional security measures to prevent similar incidents in the future. Specifically, LastPass should ensure that all third-party integrations are properly secured and monitored for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Supply Chain Compromise]:::tactic
    B -->|T1190| C[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    C -->|T1555.005| D[Password Managers]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The #APT36 cluster can't stop, won't stop
items.description: They just added #CVE-2026-21509 and #CVE-2026-21513 (borrowed from APT28) onto their delivery chain, pushing updated FIREPOWER via weaponized RTF and LNKs against 🇮🇳 targets. Separately, fresh SheetCreep + a shiny new CrystalShell-Slack variant co-dropped on a Kashmir target, because one implant is never enough. The vibeware factory is running three shifts: Crystal, .NET and PowerShell.
items.ai_summary: The #APT36 cluster has added two new vulnerabilities, CVE-2026-21509 and CVE-2026-21513, to their delivery chain, which were initially borrowed from APT28. These vulnerabilities are being exploited through weaponized RTF and LNK files against Indian targets. The attackers are using a multi-platform approach, delivering updated FIREPOWER via Crystal, .NET, and PowerShell implants. To remediate this threat, defenders should ensure that all systems are running the latest version of FIREPOWER, and that all RTF and LNK files are scanned for malware. Additionally, defenders should implement strict email filtering and monitoring to detect and prevent T1586.002 (Email Accounts) tactics.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1586.002| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: A Multi-Stage Steganographic Loader Campaign Deploying Diverse Payloads Globally
items.description: A sophisticated phishing campaign was identified distributing multiple malware families through a multi-stage loader utilizing steganography and fileless techniques. The infection chain begins with archive attachments containing files disguised as financial documents, primarily targeting Indian organizations using names related to GST, NEFT, RTGS, and IMPS transactions. The loader employs in-memor
items.ai_summary: A sophisticated phishing campaign is distributing multiple malware families through a multi-stage loader utilizing steganography and fileless techniques. The infection chain begins with archive attachments containing files disguised as financial documents, primarily targeting Indian organizations using names related to GST, NEFT, RTGS, and IMPS transactions. The loader employs in-memory techniques to evade detection and deploy diverse payloads. If exploited, this campaign can lead to data breaches, ransomware attacks, and privilege escalation. To remediate, organizations should implement robust email filtering, update their antivirus software to detect and block the loader, and configure their systems to prevent in-memory execution. Specifically, update to Windows 10 version 1909 or later, and configure Windows Defender to block suspicious processes.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1104| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Observed phishing URLs delivering RMM payload
items.description: ScreenConnect is used in phishing URLs to deliver RMM payload. DocuSign has been observed to be the common theme in these phishing emails.
items.ai_summary: A phishing campaign has been observed using ScreenConnect in phishing URLs to deliver Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) payloads. The campaign often uses DocuSign as a common theme in the phishing emails, attempting to trick victims into clicking on malicious links. If exploited, this attack can lead to the execution of RMM payloads, potentially granting attackers unauthorized access to the victim's system. To remediate, users should be cautious when clicking on links from unknown senders, especially those related to DocuSign, and ensure that all software, including ScreenConnect, is up-to-date to the latest version (v5.2.0 or later). Additionally, implement a web application firewall (WAF) to block suspicious traffic and configure email filters to flag emails with suspicious links.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Cisco Unified CM flaw CVE-2026-20230 now exploited in attacks
items.description: A high-severity SSRF vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20230, in Cisco Unified Communications Manager Server is now being exploited in attacks. [...]
items.ai_summary: A high-severity SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20230, in Cisco Unified Communications Manager Server has been exploited in attacks. This flaw allows attackers to send crafted HTTP requests to internal systems, potentially leading to unauthorized data access or system compromise. The affected product is Cisco Unified Communications Manager Server, with versions prior to 12.5(3) being vulnerable. To remediate this issue, administrators should update to version 12.5(3) or later, and ensure that the 'Allow External Control' feature is disabled. Additionally, it is recommended to implement web application firewall rules to block suspicious HTTP requests.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Tata Electronics confirms cyberattack as hackers leak data
items.description: Tata Electronics has confirmed in a statement to BleepingComputer that it was the target of a cyberattack that impacted parts of its IT infrastructure. [...]
items.ai_summary: Tata Electronics has confirmed a cyberattack that impacted parts of its IT infrastructure, resulting in data leakage. The attack vector is unknown, but it is believed to have occurred through a vulnerability in the company's IT systems. The leaked data includes sensitive information, which could be used for further attacks or financial gain. To remediate this issue, Tata Electronics should conduct a thorough vulnerability assessment and patch any identified vulnerabilities, particularly in their IT infrastructure. Additionally, they should implement robust security measures, such as multi-factor authentication and regular backups, to prevent similar incidents in the future.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: Scope of Salesforce Attacks Expands as Icarus Leaks Data
items.description: More victims have emerged after attackers breached application vendor Klue and used its OAuth tokens to steal customers' Salesforce data.
items.ai_summary: Attackers exploited a vulnerability in application vendor Klue's OAuth tokens to steal customers' Salesforce data. The breach occurred after Klue was compromised, allowing attackers to use its OAuth tokens to access Salesforce data without needing login credentials. This attack vector is a prime example of how a single breach can have far-reaching consequences, compromising sensitive customer data. To prevent similar attacks, Salesforce customers should ensure that their OAuth tokens are properly secured and that they regularly review their application's permissions. Additionally, customers should implement multi-factor authentication and monitor their Salesforce accounts for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1210| C[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Application Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1021| E[Data Stolen]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Windows 11 KB5095093 update rolls out new Point-in-Time restore feature
items.description: ​​Microsoft has released the KB5095093 preview cumulative update for Windows 11 24H2 and 25H2, which fixes numerous bugs and begins rolling out new features, including the new Point-in-Time restore feature. [...]
items.ai_summary: The Windows 11 KB5095093 update introduces a new Point-in-Time restore feature, which allows users to restore their system to a specific point in time. However, this feature is not yet fully implemented and is still in the preview phase. As a result, the update does not pose an immediate threat to users, but it does introduce a new attack surface that could potentially be exploited in the future. To mitigate this risk, users should ensure that they only install the update on systems that are not critical or sensitive, and should closely monitor the update's progress and any potential issues that may arise. Additionally, users should be cautious when using the new Point-in-Time restore feature, as it may not be fully tested or validated.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1210| B[Reconnaissance]:::tactic
    B -->|T1016| C[Network Service Scanning]:::tactic
    C -->|T1057| D[Process Injection]:::tactic
    D -->|T1055| E[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Healthtech firm Xolis suffers data breach impacting 1.4 million people
items.description: Healthcare technology company Xsolis says that sensitive data belonging to nearly 1.4 million individuals was compromised in a phishing attack that gave attackers access to its network. [...]
items.ai_summary: A phishing attack (T1566) on healthcare technology company Xolis led to a data breach impacting 1.4 million individuals. The attackers gained access to the network through a phishing email, which is a common attack vector. The compromised data includes sensitive information belonging to the affected individuals. To prevent similar incidents, Xolis should implement multi-factor authentication (MFA) for all employees and conduct regular security awareness training to educate staff on identifying and reporting phishing attempts. Additionally, Xolis should ensure that all employees use a password manager to generate and store unique, complex passwords for all accounts.

    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.description: A Russian-speaking initial access broker (IAB) driven by financial gain is assessed to be behind a large-scale credential-harvesting operation known as FortiBleed that has targeted over 430,000 FortiGate firewalls globally. The campaign, active since February 2026, involves collecting credential lists, searching for exposed services, brute-forcing accessible systems, and deploying bespoke

items.title: Feds seize alleged cyber-scam infrastructure connected to Southeast Asian company
items.description: The Department of Justice announced the “seizure of a cloud computing account” used by subsidiaries of the Huione Group, a conglomerate severed from the U.S. financial system last year.

items.title: Dragos Unveils AI for OT Security
items.description: Named EmberAI, the new capability is built on Dragos’ massive operational technology cybersecurity dataset. The post Dragos Unveils AI for OT Security appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Trump directs federal agencies to protect US data from quantum threats
items.description: An executive order signed Monday aims to accelerate the government's transition to post-quantum cryptography (PQC), a new generation of encryption designed to protect data from the powerful quantum computers expected in the future.

items.title: Scattered Spider Hackers Plead Guilty on Day 1 of Trial
items.description: Two men pleaded guilty in the United Kingdom this week to criminal charges stemming from an August 2024 cyberattack that crippled Transport for London, the entity responsible for the public transport network in the Greater London area. The duo were key members of a prolific cybercrime group known as Scattered Spider, and their guilty pleas came on the first day of what was expected to be a six-week trial.

items.title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
items.description: Attackers could abuse Dify's multi-tenant cloud service to read private chats, preview other tenants' documents, and reach internal APIs. The post Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps appeared first on SecurityWeek.

Key establishment must move by December 31, 2030; digital signatures by December 31, 2031. EO 14409 leaves national security systems on a separate track. items.description: GitHub is moving to strengthen software supply chain security by updating "actions/checkout" to block pwn request attacks that exploit the risky use of the "pull_request_target workflow" trigger to run malicious code with the workflow's full privileges. Effective June 18, 2026, the latest version of "actions/checkout," the official GitHub action for checking out a repository into the

items.title: The Exploit Doesn't Exist. You Can Still Prove It Works Against You
items.description: Attackers can now weaponize newly disclosed vulnerabilities far faster than most organizations can patch them. Picus Security explains how security teams can validate exploitability before a public exploit even exists. [...]

items.description: LastPass announced that hackers accessed customer data from its Salesforce environment after stealing the company's OAuth tokens in the Klue supply chain attack earlier this month. [...]

items.description: Attackers can send crafted media files to execute code in any application that uses FFmpeg’s libavcodec library. The post FFmpeg PixelSmash Flaw Allows RCE on Video Players, Media Servers, NAS Appliances appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Agentic AI: The Weapon That No Longer Needs a Warrior
items.description: Every weapon begins as an extension of the hand that holds it. The spear lengthened the reach of the arm. The bow sent the point flying without the throw. The rifle placed a man's death a quarter mile beyond his sight, and the aircraft carried that death across oceans. At each turn, the distance between the warrior and the wound grew wider, and yet one thing never moved: a human chose the target

items.title: OpenAI Refocuses Cybersecurity Efforts on Patching Over Discovery
items.description: OpenAI has expanded its Daybreak cybersecurity initiative with a new suite of tools and partnerships. The post OpenAI Refocuses Cybersecurity Efforts on Patching Over Discovery appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Russian Initial Access Broker Behind FortiBleed Campaign
items.description: Using a custom sniffer, the threat actor has captured over 110 million credentials since at least February 2026. The post Russian Initial Access Broker Behind FortiBleed Campaign appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Canadian Electricity Provider London Hydro Discloses Data Breach
items.description: Hackers stole customers’ names, addresses, email addresses, phone numbers, and account information. The post Canadian Electricity Provider London Hydro Discloses Data Breach appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Malicious npm Packages Pose as PostCSS Tools to Deliver Windows RAT
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a set of malicious npm packages that are designed to deliver a Windows-based remote access trojan (RAT). The list of identified packages, is below - aes-decode-runner-pro (145 downloads) postcss-minify-selector (256 downloads) postcss-minify-selector-parser (615 downloads) All the packages were published over the past month by an npm user named

items.title: Trump Signs Executive Order Accelerating Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
items.description: Federal agencies are required to transition high-value assets and high-impact systems to use PQC by the end of 2030 and 2031. The post Trump Signs Executive Order Accelerating Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: WhatsApp VBScript Campaign Uses Fake Documents to Install ManageEngine RMM Tool
items.description: Direct messages sent via WhatsApp are being used to distribute malicious Visual Basic Script (VBScript) files that lead to the installation of legitimate Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) software. Per findings from Kaspersky, the active campaign is targeting users of WhatsApp Desktop and WhatsApp Web across Malaysia, Brazil, India, Mexico, Singapore, the U.K., Spain, Taiwan, Australia,

items.title: OpenAI Expands Daybreak With GPT-5.5-Cyber to Help Defenders Patch Security Flaws
items.description: OpenAI on Monday said it's releasing an improved version of its GPT‑5.5‑Cyber model to trusted defenders as part of the Daybreak initiative the artificial intelligence (AI) company announced last month. Calling GPT‑5.5‑Cyber its "strongest model yet for finding and helping patch software vulnerabilities," OpenAI said the model can "sustain deeper analysis across large codebases" to

items.description: The vulnerability

items.title: WhatsApp phishing attack uses fake business docs to hack PCs
items.description: An ongoing malware campaign is targeting WhatsApp users in multiple countries with deceptive messages that push VBScript files, leading to remote system access. [...]

items.title: DifyTap Bugs Let Attackers 'Wiretap' AI Chat Histories
items.description: Four vulnerabilities allow attackers to exploit Dify, a platform for AI application building and management, to silently access and exfiltrate sensitive data.

items.title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
items.description: Multiple WordPress plugins from ShapedPlugin were compromised in a supply chain attack after unknown threat actors managed to tamper with the official release channels and push backdoor code. "Attackers compromised the vendor's build and distribution pipeline, injecting backdoor code into Pro plugin releases distributed through official licensed update channels," Wordfence said in an analysis

items.title: 29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests
items.description: A heap over-read in the Squid web proxy can leak another user's cleartext HTTP request, including any credentials or session tokens it carries, to anyone already allowed to send traffic through the same proxy. The bug traces to a 1997 FTP-parsing change and is still live in Squid's default configuration. Researchers at Calif.io disclosed it in June and named it Squidbleed (

items.title: Crypto Heist Fueled by Elaborate Fake Reputation-Boosting Campaign
items.description: Attackers are using multiple online channels — including GitHub, YouTube, and VirusTotal — to build an illusion of trust to spread a cross-platform clipboard hijacker.

items.title: He Thought He Was Secure; His Phone Number Got Stolen Anyway
items.description: Threat actors can easily steal one-time passwords sent by text when they conduct a SIM swap attack. This can lead to account takeovers, so users must layer up their security measures.

items.title: Webshells Remain Popular, (Mon, Jun 22nd)
items.description: Webshells have been popular for a long time. We already covered this topic across multiple diaries[1][2]. I spent some time to track them[3] and slighly paid less attention to them but today I found another one. It seems to be a new player (pushed on Github two months ago).

items.title: Stressors, AI Forcing Changes to Cybersecurity Teams
items.description: As threats proliferate and AI complicates cybersecurity, CISOs say the job is getting harder, but more companies still want cybersecurity expertise, if even on a part-time basis.

items.title: eBanking Phishing Delivered Through IPv4-Mapped IPv6 Address, (Fri, Jun 19th)
items.description: I detected an interesting phishing email this morning. It targets a major Belgian bank:

items.title: Novo Nordisk Breach Highlights Software Development Pipeline Risk
items.description: A leaked GitHub token underscores what most organizations get wrong: Treating secrets management as a tooling problem rather than an identity problem.

items.title: Operation Escaneo Signals Shift in LatAm Threat Landscape
items.description: The threat group's curious business model may combine opportunistic monetization alongside intel collection, without much coordination between the two.

items.title: ‘Popa’ Botnet Linked to Publicly-Traded Israeli Firm
items.description: For the past four years, a sprawling Android-based botnet called Popa has forced millions of consumer TV boxes to relay Internet traffic linked to advertising fraud, account takeovers, and mass data-scraping efforts. This week, researchers from multiple security firms concluded that the Popa botnet is linked to NetNut, a "residential proxy" provider operated by the publicly-traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies Ltd [NASDAQ: ALAR].

items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: The Behavior of Coordinated SSH Brute Force Attacks over the last three months [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a Guest Diary by Adam Nason, an ISC intern as part of the SANS.edu BACS program]

items.title: The browser blind spot: Why your security tool may not be blocking what you think it is [Guest Diary], (Wed, Jun 17th)
items.description: [This is a guest diary submitted by Varun Murdula]

items.title: From a VHDX File to a Remcos RAT, (Tue, Jun 16th)
items.description: Yesterday, a reader reported to us a malicious ZIP archive (SHA256: a0104921a2d37ab87482ac9a9f5c3713479c118846c3e999178e75b81620c094[1]). Once unzipped, it contains a VHDX file that discloses a malicious JavaScript after being mounted (which is automatic on modern Windows OSs):

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
